question: The docs warn against 'overspecifying' a process test. Which rule of thumb do they recommend you always follow when writing the assertions?

options.text: Always assert that the expected external effects (e.g. business service calls) really happened; pick a small set of internal-state assertions only where they truly matter.
Always assert every visited element id in order so any future model change is caught immediately.
Always assert that every variable in scope still holds its initial value; deviations indicate model drift.
Always assert that the process completes within a fixed wall-clock duration so performance regressions surface in tests.

a.text: The docs state this rule of thumb directly: always assert that the expected external effects of your process really took place (e.g. that business services were called as expected), and carefully choose which aspects of internal process state are important enough to warn about later.

b.text: Asserting every visited element is the textbook overspecification the docs warn against — the process definition will evolve, and such tests break for every model change rather than for real regressions.

c.text: Pinning every variable value is even more brittle and is not the docs' guidance.

d.text: Wall-clock duration is a performance concern; the docs' rule of thumb is about external effects vs internal state, not timing.

explanation: The docs advise treating external effects as the assertion bedrock — those reflect what the process is contractually doing for the outside world — and being selective about internal-state assertions so the test breaks only when something meaningful changes, not for every BPMN tweak.
